choiceExplanations.D: This would require only one enantiomer to be present.

questions.commonMistake: Reporting percent of the major enantiomer rather than enantiomeric excess.

questions.stem: Which control best tests whether enantioseparation depends on the chiral stationary phase rather than only on pH?

choices.text: Run the same mixture on an achiral column under the same pH and salt conditions.
Increase detector brightness while keeping the same column.
Inject only solvent and no compound C.
Measure the density of the mobile phase without chromatography.

questions.explanation: To isolate the role of the chiral stationary phase, the pH and salt conditions should be held constant while column chirality is changed. If the achiral column does not separate the enantiomers under the same conditions, the chiral stationary phase is implicated.

choiceExplanations.A: This changes the variable of interest while controlling mobile-phase conditions.
choiceExplanations.B: Detector brightness can affect signal intensity but does not test the mechanism of separation.
choiceExplanations.C: A solvent blank checks contamination but cannot test enantioseparation.
choiceExplanations.D: Mobile-phase density does not test the role of chiral recognition.

questions.commonMistake: Using a blank control when the key question requires isolating the independent variable responsible for separation.

questions.stem: A 0.50 mol sample of an ideal monatomic gas is heated by 20 K at constant pressure. Approximately how much heat is added? Use Cp = 5R/2 and R = 8.3 J mol^-1 K^-1.
questions.explanation: At constant pressure, q = nCpDeltaT. Cp = 5R/2 = 20.75 J mol^-1 K^-1. Thus q = 0.50 x 20.75 x 20 = about 208 J.

choiceExplanations.A: This resembles nRDeltaT, the expansion work term, not the constant-pressure heat added.
choiceExplanations.B: This is too low and does not use the full constant-pressure heat capacity.
choiceExplanations.C: This correctly uses q = nCpDeltaT.
choiceExplanations.D: This omits the factor of 0.50 mol.

questions.commonMistake: Using R or Cv when the process is explicitly at constant pressure.

questions.stem: A 6 microF capacitor and a 3 microF capacitor are connected in series across a 9 V battery. What is the voltage across the 3 microF capacitor?
questions.explanation: For capacitors in series, the charge on each capacitor is the same. The equivalent capacitance is 2 microF, so Q = CeqV = 18 microC. The voltage across the 3 microF capacitor is V = Q/C = 18 microC/3 microF = 6 V.

choiceExplanations.A: This is the voltage across the 6 microF capacitor, not the 3 microF capacitor.
choiceExplanations.B: This assumes the voltage splits equally, which occurs only for equal capacitances in series.
choiceExplanations.C: This correctly applies equal series charge and V = Q/C.
choiceExplanations.D: This incorrectly assigns the full battery voltage to one capacitor.

questions.commonMistake: Assuming capacitors in series split voltage equally regardless of capacitance.

questions.stem: A weak monoprotic acid HA is titrated with strong base. At the half-equivalence point, the measured pH is 5.20. What is the acid's Ka?
questions.explanation: At the half-equivalence point of a weak acid-strong base titration, [A-] = [HA], so pH = pKa. Therefore pKa = 5.20 and Ka = 10^-5.20 = about 6.3 x 10^-6.

choiceExplanations.A: This correctly uses pH = pKa at half-equivalence.
choiceExplanations.B: This treats the pH value as if it could be directly converted without the negative exponent relation.
choiceExplanations.C: This corresponds to pKa near 4.8, not 5.20.
choiceExplanations.D: This incorrectly squares or doubles the pKa relationship.

questions.commonMistake: Confusing half-equivalence with equivalence and not recognizing that pH equals pKa at half-equivalence.

questions.stem: A 0.500 A current passes through molten Al3+ for 30.0 min. If all current deposits aluminum metal according to Al3+ + 3e- -> Al, what mass of Al is deposited? Use F = 96500 C/mol e- and Al = 27.0 g/mol.
questions.explanation: Charge passed is Q = It = 0.500 A x 1800 s = 900 C. Moles of electrons are 900/96500 = 0.00933 mol e-. Since 3 mol e- deposit 1 mol Al, moles Al = 0.00311 mol. The mass is 0.00311 x 27.0 = 0.084 g.

choiceExplanations.A: This divides by 9 rather than by the 3-electron stoichiometry.
choiceExplanations.B: This correctly applies charge, Faraday's constant, and the 3-electron reduction.
choiceExplanations.C: This reports the mass expected if each electron deposited one aluminum atom.
choiceExplanations.D: This is not consistent with the charge passed and stoichiometry.

questions.commonMistake: Ignoring that Al3+ requires three electrons per atom deposited.

questions.stem: For a real object placed in front of a diverging lens, which image description is always correct?
questions.explanation: A diverging lens causes rays from a real object to diverge as though they came from a point on the same side as the object. The image is virtual, upright, and reduced for any real object position.

choiceExplanations.A: A diverging lens does not form a real inverted image of a real object by itself.
choiceExplanations.B: The image is upright and reduced, but it is virtual, not real.
choiceExplanations.C: This correctly describes images formed by diverging lenses for real objects.
choiceExplanations.D: The image is upright and reduced, not inverted and magnified.

questions.commonMistake: Applying converging-lens image rules to a diverging lens.

questions.stem: Which reactant limits CO2 production in Trial C?

choices.text: NaHCO3, because it is present in fewer stoichiometric equivalents than H+
H+, because acid always limits gas production
CO2, because it is initially negligible
Neither reactant, because gases do not follow stoichiometry

questions.explanation: The reaction is 1:1 between bicarbonate and H+. Trial C contains 0.50 mmol NaHCO3 and 1.00 mmol H+, so bicarbonate is limiting. This explains why Trial C produces about the same CO2 volume as Trial B.

choiceExplanations.A: This correctly applies the 1:1 stoichiometry in the passage.
choiceExplanations.B: Acid is in excess in Trial C, so it is not limiting.
choiceExplanations.C: CO2 is a product, not a limiting reactant.
choiceExplanations.D: Gas-forming reactions still obey balanced chemical stoichiometry.

questions.commonMistake: Choosing the stronger or more reactive-looking substance instead of comparing stoichiometric amounts.

questions.stem: Which observation best supports the 1:1 stoichiometry of HCO3- reacting with H+ to form CO2?

choices.text: Trial B produces about twice the CO2 volume of Trial A when acid is increased from 0.25 mmol to 0.50 mmol with bicarbonate still sufficient.
Trial C produces less CO2 than Trial A despite having more acid.
Trial D produces the same CO2 volume as Trial A despite having more of both reactants.
All trials produce exactly the same CO2 volume.

questions.explanation: Trial A has 0.25 mmol acid and Trial B has 0.50 mmol acid, while bicarbonate is not in excess in both cases relative to acid. The CO2 volume approximately doubles from 6.0 mL to 12.1 mL, consistent with 1 mol CO2 formed per 1 mol H+ consumed when bicarbonate is available.

choiceExplanations.A: This correctly identifies the controlled increase that supports the reaction stoichiometry.
choiceExplanations.B: Trial C does not produce less CO2 than Trial A.
choiceExplanations.C: Trial D produces about four times Trial A's volume, not the same volume.
choiceExplanations.D: The data show different volumes across trials.

questions.commonMistake: Comparing trials where both limiting status and reactant amounts change in a way that obscures the stoichiometric relationship.

questions.stem: In Trial D, approximately how much work is done by the CO2 gas on the syringe plunger as it expands against 1.00 atm?
questions.explanation: Work at constant external pressure is W = PDeltaV. Trial D produces 24.1 mL = 2.41 x 10^-5 m3 of gas. With P = 1.01 x 10^5 Pa, W is about 2.4 J.

choiceExplanations.A: This is too small by about a factor of 100 and likely mishandles mL-to-m3 conversion.
choiceExplanations.B: This is too small by about a factor of 10.
choiceExplanations.C: This correctly uses pressure-volume work with SI units.
choiceExplanations.D: This is too large by about a factor of 10.

questions.commonMistake: Using pressure-volume work without converting milliliters to cubic meters.

questions.stem: What is the focal length of the converging lens in the standard optical setup?
questions.explanation: The thin-lens equation is 1/f = 1/do + 1/di. With do = 18 cm and di = 36 cm, 1/f = 1/18 + 1/36 = 3/36 = 1/12, so f = 12 cm.

choiceExplanations.A: This is too short and may result from adding distances incorrectly.
choiceExplanations.B: This correctly applies the thin-lens equation.
choiceExplanations.C: This incorrectly assumes the focal length equals the object distance.
choiceExplanations.D: This incorrectly adds object and image distances.

questions.commonMistake: Using f = do + di rather than the reciprocal thin-lens relationship.

questions.stem: Which negative control would best test whether measured gas volume is due to bicarbonate reaction rather than air leakage or syringe drift?

choices.text: Run the same procedure with acid but no NaHCO3.
Use twice as much NaHCO3 and twice as much acid.
Increase the camera magnification without changing the chemical mixture.
Record only the final plunger position and not the initial position.

questions.explanation: A negative control should be treated the same way as the experimental sample but omit the reactant necessary for CO2 generation. Acid without bicarbonate should produce no CO2 from the stated reaction, so any measured volume would suggest leakage, drift, or another artifact.

choiceExplanations.A: This omits the bicarbonate reactant while preserving the handling conditions.
choiceExplanations.B: This is another positive reaction condition, not a negative control.
choiceExplanations.C: Changing optical magnification tests readout sensitivity, not chemical gas generation.
choiceExplanations.D: Not recording the initial position weakens the measurement and does not provide a control.

questions.commonMistake: Using a stronger reaction condition as a control instead of omitting the key reactant.

questions.stem: A beta-keto carboxylic acid is heated and releases CO2. Which structural feature most directly explains its relatively facile decarboxylation?

choices.text: The carboxyl group is positioned so that a cyclic transition state can form while producing an enol.
All carboxylic acids decarboxylate rapidly at room temperature.
The molecule lacks any carbonyl group other than the carboxylic acid.
The carboxyl carbon has no partial positive character.

questions.explanation: Beta-keto acids can decarboxylate relatively readily because the beta carbonyl helps organize a cyclic transition state and stabilize enol formation after CO2 loss. Ordinary carboxylic acids do not all decarboxylate easily under mild conditions.

choiceExplanations.A: This identifies the key structural reason beta-keto acids decarboxylate readily.
choiceExplanations.B: This is false; many carboxylic acids are stable at room temperature.
choiceExplanations.C: A beta-keto acid specifically contains an additional ketone carbonyl.
choiceExplanations.D: The carboxyl carbon is electrophilic due to the carbonyl and attached heteroatoms.

questions.commonMistake: Overgeneralizing beta-keto acid behavior to all carboxylic acids.

questions.stem: Two positive point charges have electric potential energy U when separated by distance r. If their separation is increased to 2r, what is the new electric potential energy?
questions.explanation: Electric potential energy for two point charges is U = kq1q2/r. Doubling the separation halves the potential energy, so the new value is U/2.

choiceExplanations.A: This incorrectly applies an inverse-square relationship, which applies to force magnitude, not potential energy.
choiceExplanations.B: This correctly uses the inverse-distance relationship for electrostatic potential energy.
choiceExplanations.C: This reverses the effect of increasing separation.
choiceExplanations.D: This incorrectly makes energy increase with the square of distance.

questions.commonMistake: Confusing the 1/r potential energy dependence with the 1/r^2 force dependence.

questions.stem: A cylindrical wire is replaced by one made of the same material with twice the length and half the radius. By what factor does its resistance change?

choices.text: It decreases by a factor of 2.
It increases by a factor of 2.
It increases by a factor of 4.
It increases by a factor of 8.

questions.explanation: Resistance is R = rho L/A. Doubling length doubles resistance. Halving radius reduces cross-sectional area by a factor of 4, which increases resistance by a factor of 4. The total factor is 2 x 4 = 8.

choiceExplanations.A: This reverses the effect of increasing length and decreasing area.
choiceExplanations.B: This accounts only for the length change.
choiceExplanations.C: This accounts only for the area change.
choiceExplanations.D: This correctly combines both geometry changes.

questions.commonMistake: Forgetting that cross-sectional area depends on radius squared.

questions.stem: A mixture contains two miscible neutral liquids with boiling points of 78 C and 82 C. Which method is most appropriate for separating them based primarily on volatility?
questions.explanation: Fractional distillation is appropriate for separating miscible liquids with relatively close boiling points because the fractionating column allows repeated vaporization-condensation cycles that enrich the more volatile component.

choiceExplanations.A: This is the best volatility-based method for liquids with close boiling points.
choiceExplanations.B: Filtration separates solids from fluids, not miscible liquids.
choiceExplanations.C: Acid-base extraction requires an acid-base difference, not merely different boiling points.
choiceExplanations.D: Dialysis separates species by membrane permeability and size, not volatility.

questions.commonMistake: Choosing simple mechanical separation methods for a homogeneous liquid mixture.

questions.stem: An organic acid contains 40.0% C, 6.7% H, and 53.3% O by mass. Its molar mass is approximately 90 g/mol. What is its molecular formula?
questions.explanation: Assume 100 g: C = 40.0/12 = 3.33 mol, H = 6.7/1 = 6.7 mol, O = 53.3/16 = 3.33 mol. Dividing by 3.33 gives CH2O, empirical mass 30 g/mol. With molar mass 90 g/mol, the molecular formula is three empirical units: C3H6O3.

choiceExplanations.A: This is the empirical formula, not the molecular formula.
choiceExplanations.B: This has molar mass 60 g/mol, not 90 g/mol.
choiceExplanations.C: This correctly multiplies the empirical formula by 3.
choiceExplanations.D: This has the same empirical formula but molar mass 180 g/mol.

questions.commonMistake: Stopping at the empirical formula and ignoring the molar mass.

questions.stem: Why is lactate removal much greater at pH 7.0 than at pH 3.0 when comparing Runs 1 and 2?

choices.text: At pH 7.0, lactate is predominantly negatively charged and migrates toward the anode.
At pH 7.0, lactate is predominantly neutral and therefore moves faster in the electric field.
At pH 3.0, all lactate molecules carry two negative charges.
At pH 3.0, the anion-exchange membrane becomes a cathode.

questions.explanation: Lactic acid has pKa 3.9. At pH 7.0, most molecules are deprotonated as lactate anions, which migrate through the anion-exchange membrane toward the positive anode. At pH 3.0, more molecules are neutral lactic acid and are less affected by the electric field.

choiceExplanations.A: This correctly connects pH, charge state, and electrophoretic direction.
choiceExplanations.B: Neutral molecules do not migrate efficiently in an electric field.
choiceExplanations.C: Lactate is singly charged when deprotonated, and pH 3.0 favors the neutral acid more than pH 7.0.
choiceExplanations.D: The membrane does not become an electrode because of pH.

questions.commonMistake: Failing to link acid-base speciation to electric-field-driven transport.

questions.stem: What is the effective electrical resistance of the device during Run 2?
questions.explanation: Ohm's law gives R = V/I. In Run 2, V = 20 V and I = 10.0 mA = 0.010 A, so R = 20/0.010 = 2000 ohm = 2.0 kohm.

choiceExplanations.A: This would correspond to 40 mA at 20 V, not the listed current.
choiceExplanations.B: This correctly applies Ohm's law with milliamperes converted to amperes.
choiceExplanations.C: This is too large by a factor of 10.
choiceExplanations.D: This is too large by a factor of 100.

questions.commonMistake: Forgetting to convert milliamperes to amperes before calculating resistance.

questions.stem: Run 5 has higher current than Run 2 but lower lactate removal. Which explanation is most consistent with the passage?

choices.text: High salt introduces competing ions that carry current and increase heating without selectively transporting lactate.
Higher current always proves that more lactate is transported.
Lactate becomes positively charged in high salt at pH 7.0.
High salt eliminates all electric fields in the chamber.

questions.explanation: The passage states that current may be carried by lactate and by other buffer ions. Run 5's high salt condition increases current and temperature rise but shows lower lactate removal, consistent with competing ions carrying much of the current and with heating reducing separation efficiency.

choiceExplanations.A: This accounts for both the elevated current and reduced selectivity.
choiceExplanations.B: The data directly contradict this claim.
choiceExplanations.C: Lactate remains a negatively charged carboxylate at pH 7.0.
choiceExplanations.D: A current is measured, so the electric field is not eliminated.

questions.commonMistake: Treating total current as equivalent to selective transport of the analyte of interest.

questions.stem: What conclusion is best supported by comparing Runs 3 and 4?

choices.text: Cooling improves lactate removal under the same voltage and current conditions.
Cooling reverses the charge of lactate.
Lower temperature rise eliminates the need for an anion-exchange membrane.
Voltage has no effect on lactate removal.

questions.explanation: Runs 3 and 4 have the same pH, voltage, current, and low-salt condition. Run 4 uses cooling, has a smaller temperature rise, and removes more lactate. This supports the conclusion that reducing Joule heating improves separation performance under these conditions.

choiceExplanations.A: This is the controlled conclusion supported by the comparison.
choiceExplanations.B: Cooling does not change lactate from an anion to a cation.
choiceExplanations.C: The membrane is still part of the device and remains relevant for separation.
choiceExplanations.D: This comparison does not test voltage because voltage is held constant.

questions.commonMistake: Making a broad conclusion about voltage from a comparison in which voltage is controlled rather than varied.

questions.stem: In Run 2, approximately what is the minimum charge associated with the lactate ions removed from the feed chamber, assuming each lactate ion carries one elementary charge?
questions.explanation: Initial total lactate species are (0.100 mM)(0.100 L) = 0.0100 mmol = 1.00 x 10^-5 mol. Run 2 removes 45%, or 4.5 x 10^-6 mol lactate. For singly charged lactate, charge = nF = 4.5 x 10^-6 x 96500 = about 0.43 C.

choiceExplanations.A: This is too small by a factor of 10.
choiceExplanations.B: This correctly calculates removed moles and multiplies by Faraday's constant.
choiceExplanations.C: This is too large by a factor of 10.
choiceExplanations.D: This is too large by a factor of 100.

questions.commonMistake: Mixing up millimolar, millimoles, and moles in a charge-transport calculation.

questions.stem: A gas expands from 2.0 L to 5.0 L against a constant external pressure of 1.0 atm. What is the magnitude of the pressure-volume work done by the gas?
questions.explanation: The volume change is 3.0 L. Work magnitude is PDeltaV = 3.0 L atm. Since 1 L atm is about 101 J, the work is about 303 J.

choiceExplanations.A: This uses only 1 L atm rather than the 3 L change.
choiceExplanations.B: This correctly calculates the magnitude of expansion work.
choiceExplanations.C: This incorrectly uses the final volume rather than the change in volume.
choiceExplanations.D: This incorrectly uses the sum of initial and final volumes.

questions.commonMistake: Using final volume instead of change in volume for pressure-volume work.

questions.stem: In a galvanic Zn/Cu cell, Zn is oxidized at the anode and Cu2+ is reduced at the cathode. Which statement correctly describes ion migration in the salt bridge?

choices.text: Anions migrate toward the anode, and cations migrate toward the cathode.
Anions migrate toward the cathode, and cations migrate toward the anode.
Both anions and cations migrate toward the cathode.
Only electrons move through the salt bridge.

questions.explanation: At the anode, oxidation of zinc produces Zn2+, creating excess positive charge, so anions from the salt bridge migrate toward the anode. At the cathode, Cu2+ is consumed, so cations migrate toward the cathode to maintain charge balance.

choiceExplanations.A: This correctly describes salt bridge ion migration in a galvanic cell.
choiceExplanations.B: This reverses the ion migration directions.
choiceExplanations.C: Both ion types do not migrate to the same half-cell for charge balance.
choiceExplanations.D: Electrons move through the external wire, not through the salt bridge.

questions.commonMistake: Confusing electron flow in the wire with ion migration in the salt bridge.

questions.stem: A weak acid buffer has the greatest resistance to pH change when which condition is met?

choices.text: The weak acid concentration is zero.
The conjugate base concentration is zero.
The weak acid and conjugate base concentrations are approximately equal.
The pH is at least 5 units above the pKa.

questions.explanation: Buffer capacity is greatest when both weak acid and conjugate base are present in comparable amounts, typically near pH = pKa. Then the solution can neutralize added acid or added base effectively.

choiceExplanations.A: Without weak acid, the solution cannot effectively neutralize added base.
choiceExplanations.B: Without conjugate base, the solution cannot effectively neutralize added acid.
choiceExplanations.C: This correctly describes the condition for maximum buffer capacity.
choiceExplanations.D: Far above the pKa, the acid form is depleted and buffer capacity is poor.

questions.commonMistake: Thinking that the strongest buffering occurs when one component greatly dominates the other.

questions.stem: A corrective diverging lens has focal length -50 cm. What is its lens power?
questions.explanation: Lens power is P = 1/f when f is in meters. A focal length of -50 cm is -0.50 m, so P = 1/(-0.50) = -2.0 diopters.

choiceExplanations.A: This correctly converts centimeters to meters and keeps the negative sign for a diverging lens.
choiceExplanations.B: This uses centimeters incorrectly or takes the reciprocal of 2 rather than 0.50 m.
choiceExplanations.C: This has the wrong sign and magnitude.
choiceExplanations.D: This has the correct magnitude but wrong sign for a diverging lens.

questions.commonMistake: Calculating lens power without converting focal length to meters.

questions.stem: In SDS-PAGE, proteins are treated with sodium dodecyl sulfate before electrophoresis. What is the main reason this treatment allows separation primarily by molecular mass?

choices.text: SDS gives proteins a roughly uniform negative charge-to-mass ratio.
SDS converts all proteins into neutral molecules.
SDS causes smaller proteins to have lower charge density than larger proteins.
SDS separates proteins by boiling point.

questions.explanation: SDS denatures proteins and coats them with negative charge in approximate proportion to length. This makes charge-to-mass ratios more uniform, so migration through the gel depends mainly on size, with smaller proteins moving more readily.

choiceExplanations.A: This correctly explains the mass-based separation principle of SDS-PAGE.
choiceExplanations.B: SDS gives proteins negative charge rather than neutralizing them.
choiceExplanations.C: The purpose is to make charge-to-mass ratios more similar, not systematically lower for smaller proteins.
choiceExplanations.D: Electrophoresis separates by motion in an electric field through a matrix, not by volatility.

questions.commonMistake: Assuming electrophoretic migration always reflects native charge rather than recognizing the role of SDS treatment.

questions.stem: At pH 5.8, what is the approximate ratio of deprotonated R to protonated R?
questions.explanation: The Henderson-Hasselbalch relationship gives pH = pKa + log([R-]/[HR]). At pH 5.8 and pKa 4.8, the pH is 1.0 unit above pKa, so [R-]/[HR] = 10. This matches the high deprotonated fraction in the table.

choiceExplanations.A: This reverses the ratio expected when pH is above pKa.
choiceExplanations.B: A 1:1 ratio occurs at pH = pKa, not one unit above pKa.
choiceExplanations.C: This underestimates the effect of one full pH unit.
choiceExplanations.D: This correctly applies the Henderson-Hasselbalch relationship.

questions.commonMistake: Treating a one-unit pH difference as a linear factor rather than a tenfold ratio.

questions.stem: At pH 5.8, what current flows through the solution when the 5.0 V source is applied?
questions.explanation: At pH 5.8, the resistance is 2.5 kohm = 2500 ohm. Ohm's law gives I = V/R = 5.0 V/2500 ohm = 0.0020 A = 2.0 mA.

choiceExplanations.A: This is too small by a factor of 10.
choiceExplanations.B: This correctly uses Ohm's law and converts amperes to milliamperes.
choiceExplanations.C: This incorrectly multiplies voltage and resistance-related values.
choiceExplanations.D: This is much too large for a 2.5 kohm resistance at 5.0 V.

questions.commonMistake: Forgetting that kilo-ohms are thousands of ohms.

questions.stem: If the actuator gas warms from 298 K to 313 K at constant pressure, approximately how much expansion work is done by the gas against 1.00 atm?
questions.explanation: At constant pressure, volume is proportional to absolute temperature. DeltaV = 2.00 mL x (313 - 298)/298 = about 0.101 mL = 1.01 x 10^-7 m3. Work is PDeltaV = (1.01 x 10^5 Pa)(1.01 x 10^-7 m3) = about 0.010 J.

choiceExplanations.A: This is too small by about a factor of 10.
choiceExplanations.B: This correctly combines Charles's law with pressure-volume work.
choiceExplanations.C: This is too large by about a factor of 10.
choiceExplanations.D: This is far too large for a 0.101 mL expansion at 1 atm.

questions.commonMistake: Using the final gas volume rather than the volume change in the work calculation.

questions.stem: In the optical system, the droplet initially sits 20 cm from the lens and forms a sharp image 20 cm from the lens. If the droplet moves to 15 cm from the lens, where should the camera be moved to maintain focus?
questions.explanation: Using 1/f = 1/do + 1/di with f = 10 cm and do = 15 cm gives 1/di = 1/10 - 1/15 = 1/30. Thus di = 30 cm, so the camera should move farther from the lens.

choiceExplanations.A: The image would not form at the focal point for an object outside the focal length.
choiceExplanations.B: This assumes the image distance equals the object distance, which is true only at 2f.
choiceExplanations.C: This correctly applies the thin-lens equation after the object moves closer.
choiceExplanations.D: This overestimates the required image distance.

questions.commonMistake: Assuming that moving the object closer always moves the image closer for a converging lens when the object remains outside the focal length.

questions.stem: Which additional measurement would best test whether the conductivity increase is specifically due to ester hydrolysis producing carboxylic acid R rather than unrelated salt contamination?

choices.text: Use chromatography to show that Q decreases while R increases over time using authentic standards.
Measure only the color of the buffer after 20 min.
Increase the applied voltage until all samples have the same current.
Omit the ion-exchange recovery step and assume all ions are R.

questions.explanation: Conductivity is not chemically specific; any dissolved ions could increase current. A chromatographic assay that tracks loss of ester Q and formation of carboxylic acid R using standards would directly test whether hydrolysis is occurring.

choiceExplanations.A: This provides chemically specific evidence for conversion of Q to R.
choiceExplanations.B: Color is not a reliable or specific measure of ester hydrolysis here.
choiceExplanations.C: Forcing currents to match would obscure conductivity differences and would not identify the chemical species.
choiceExplanations.D: Assuming all ions are R is exactly the unsupported inference the experiment should test.

questions.commonMistake: Treating a nonspecific electrical signal as chemically specific without an orthogonal analytical check.
